entries.title: Built-in hybrid caching, faster runtime, better AOT
entries.bodyMarkdown:
**HybridCache** finally lands as a first-class API — L1 (in-memory) + L2 (Redis) with **stampede protection built in** (the per-key locking you'd otherwise hand-roll). Register with `AddHybridCache()`.

Other highlights:
- **Server GC** dynamic adaptation — lower memory under variable load
- **`System.Text.Json`** gains nullable-annotation respect + faster serialization
- **LINQ** adds `CountBy` and `AggregateBy` (group-and-count in one pass)
- Better **Native AOT** coverage for ASP.NET Core minimal APIs

**Learn next:** replace any hand-rolled `IMemoryCache` + Redis two-level cache with HybridCache.

entries.title: Stay on a current LTS (see the live posts above for the newest)
entries.bodyMarkdown:
Target the current **LTS** release — .NET 8 and .NET 9 reach end of support on **Nov 10, 2026**, so migrate to the latest LTS (**.NET 10**). Features worth knowing that landed across recent versions:
- **Keyed DI services** — `AddKeyedSingleton` + `[FromKeyedServices]` (the named-registration many of us faked before)
- **`TimeProvider`** abstraction — testable time, finally
- **`IExceptionHandler`** for clean centralized error handling
- Native AOT for APIs; **Blazor** unified render modes

*The 📡 Latest posts above always show the newest official announcements.*

entries.title: Built-in resilience + rate limiting are now standard
entries.bodyMarkdown:
Two things that used to need extra packages are now first-party:
- **`AddStandardResilienceHandler()`** on HttpClient (Microsoft.Extensions.Resilience over Polly) — retry + circuit breaker + timeout preconfigured
- **Rate limiting middleware** (`AddRateLimiter`) with token-bucket/sliding-window partitions

If your services still call HttpClient with no timeout or breaker, this is the upgrade.

entries.title: .NET Aspire — orchestration for cloud-native apps
entries.bodyMarkdown:
**.NET Aspire** is Microsoft's opinionated stack for wiring multi-service apps (API + Redis + Postgres + workers) with a dashboard, service discovery, and OpenTelemetry baked in. Worth a look if you're moving from a monolith toward multiple services and want the plumbing handled.

**Learn next:** spin up the Aspire starter template and watch the dashboard trace a request across services.

entries.title: params collections, new lock type, cleaner code
entries.bodyMarkdown:
- **`params` collections** — `params ReadOnlySpan<T>` and any collection type, not just arrays (fewer allocations on hot paths)
- **`System.Threading.Lock`** — a dedicated lock type faster than `lock(object)` and analyzer-guarded
- **`\e`** escape for ESC, partial properties, implicit index in initializers (`^1`)

**Learn next:** switch hot-path `params object[]` methods to `params ReadOnlySpan<T>`.

entries.title: Primary constructors everywhere + collection expressions
entries.bodyMarkdown:
The two features you'll use daily (this app is written with them):
- **Primary constructors on any class** — `class OrderService(IRepo repo)` — no more boilerplate field assignment. Great with DI.
- **Collection expressions** — `int[] x = [1, 2, 3];` and the spread `[..a, ..b]` — one syntax for arrays, lists, spans.
- **Alias any type** with `using`, default lambda parameters.

entries.title: Modern C# leans on patterns — master them
entries.bodyMarkdown:
Across recent versions, **pattern matching** has become the idiomatic way to branch: list patterns (`[1, .., 9]`), property patterns (`{ Status: Open, Total: > 1000 }`), relational + logical patterns (`is > 0 and < 100`), and `switch` expressions with exhaustiveness. They often replace Visitor and long if/else chains.

**Learn next:** rewrite one status-switch method as a `switch` expression with a `_ => throw` arm for exhaustiveness.

entries.title: Turn on <Nullable>enable</Nullable> if you haven't
entries.bodyMarkdown:
Nullable reference types (`?` / `!`, flow analysis) catch the billion-dollar mistake at compile time. New projects have it on by default; legacy ones should migrate file-by-file with `#nullable enable`. Combined with `required` members, the compiler enforces initialization.

**Learn next:** enable nullable on one legacy project and fix the first 10 warnings — each is a latent NullReferenceException.

entries.title: Signals stabilize, incremental hydration, standalone by default
entries.bodyMarkdown:
- **`linkedSignal`** and **`resource()`** — writable-derived signals and async-data-as-signal (loading/error/value states built in)
- **Incremental hydration** — `@defer (hydrate on viewport)` ships SSR HTML and hydrates islands lazily
- **Standalone components are the default** — the CLI stops generating NgModules
- Route-level render mode config for SSR/SSG per route

**Learn next:** replace a manual HTTP-loading component with `resource()` or `httpResource()`.

entries.title: Zoneless preview + Material 3
entries.bodyMarkdown:
- **Experimental zoneless change detection** (`provideZonelessChangeDetection()`) — drop zone.js and rely on signals for fine-grained updates
- **Angular Material 3** design tokens
- **`@let`** in templates — local template variables at last
- Unified control flow (`@if/@for/@switch`) now stable and the recommended default over `*ngIf/*ngFor`

entries.title: The reactivity model to learn now
entries.bodyMarkdown:
Signals are the direction of the framework: `signal()`, `computed()`, `effect()`, plus signal-based `input()`, `output()`, `model()` for components, and `toSignal`/`toObservable` for RxJS interop.

Division of labor: **signals for state, RxJS for events over time** (debounce, switchMap, retry). Stop using `BehaviorSubject` as 'state with .value' — that's exactly what a signal is.

**Learn next:** convert one BehaviorSubject-based service to a signals store (private writable + public readonly + computed).

entries.title: @for now requires track — for good reason
entries.bodyMarkdown:
The built-in `@for` makes **`track` mandatory**, learning from a decade of `*ngFor` without `trackBy` being the #1 perf review comment. `track item.id` reuses DOM rows across immutable array replacements (which OnPush and signals require), instead of destroying and recreating everything.

**Learn next:** migrate templates with `ng generate @angular/core:control-flow`.

entries.title: Decorators, const type params, using declarations
entries.bodyMarkdown:
- **Stage-3 decorators** (5.0) — the standardized version, no more `experimentalDecorators` for new code
- **`const` type parameters** (5.0) — `function f<const T>(x: T)` infers literal types without `as const`
- **`using` / `await using`** (5.2) — deterministic disposal (`Symbol.dispose`), like C#'s `using`
- **`satisfies`** operator — validate a value against a type without widening it

entries.title: Make illegal states unrepresentable
entries.bodyMarkdown:
The single highest-leverage TS pattern: model async UI state as `{ status: 'loading' } | { status: 'error', message } | { status: 'loaded', data }` instead of a bag of optional booleans. The compiler narrows by the discriminant — no `!`, no `?.`, and an exhaustive `switch` with a `never` check catches unhandled cases.

**Learn next:** refactor one component's loading/error/data booleans into a discriminated union.

entries.title: unknown over any; strict: true always
entries.bodyMarkdown:
`any` disables checking and infects everything it touches; **`unknown`** forces narrowing before use — the right type for external/HTTP data. Keep `strict: true` (Angular's default) — `strictNullChecks` alone deletes a whole bug class. Every `as` is a promise the compiler can't verify; prefer narrowing.

**Learn next:** grep your code for `: any` and replace the external-data ones with `unknown` + validation (zod or manual guards).

entries.title: Parameter-sensitive plans, ledger, and cloud links
entries.bodyMarkdown:
- **Parameter Sensitive Plan (PSP) optimization** — multiple cached plans per query bucketed by cardinality. This is the built-in cure for the parameter-sniffing bomb (needs dbcompat 160).
- **Intelligent Query Processing** upgrades: memory-grant feedback persistence, cardinality estimation feedback, degree-of-parallelism feedback
- **Ledger tables** — cryptographically verifiable, tamper-evident data
- **Azure Synapse Link** and managed-instance link for near-real-time replication

**Learn next:** enable dbcompat 160 on a dev DB and watch PSP create multiple plans for a skewed-parameter query.

entries.title: Native vector search + AI integration
entries.bodyMarkdown:
The big theme is **AI in the database**: a native **`vector` data type** with approximate nearest-neighbor search, built-in embedding generation, and hybrid keyword+vector search — so semantic search lives next to your relational data instead of a separate vector store.

**Learn next:** read up on the `vector` type and how hybrid search (BM25 + cosine) is becoming the default for product/document search.

entries.title: Your regression flight recorder — use it
entries.bodyMarkdown:
Query Store records queries, plans, and runtime stats over time — on by default since 2022. It answers 'what got slower and when', and **plan forcing** is the surgical tourniquet for a sniffing regression at 2 AM. Also powers automatic plan correction (`FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN`).

**Learn next:** open the Regressed Queries report in SSMS on any busy database and force a plan.

entries.title: Complex types, primitive collections, better JSON
entries.bodyMarkdown:
- **Complex types** (owned-but-not-entity value objects like `Address`) — no hidden key, proper value semantics
- **Primitive collections** — map `List<string>`/`int[]` to a JSON column and even `Contains` over them translates to SQL
- **`ExecuteUpdate`/`ExecuteDelete`** (EF7+) — set-based bulk updates without loading entities, the fix for tracked-loop mass updates
- EF9: richer **JSON column** querying, better complex-type support, `LEFT JOIN` improvements

**Learn next:** replace one load-modify-SaveChanges mass update with `ExecuteUpdateAsync`.

entries.title: Projection, AsNoTracking, split queries
entries.bodyMarkdown:
The highest-impact EF habits never change: **project to DTOs with `Select`** (only needed columns, untracked, one query — kills N+1 and over-fetching), **`AsNoTracking`** for reads, and **`AsSplitQuery`** when including two+ collections to avoid cartesian explosion. Always inspect the SQL with `ToQueryString()`.

**Learn next:** turn on `LogTo` and find one endpoint doing N+1, then fix it with projection.

entries.title: Faster startup for big DbContexts
entries.bodyMarkdown:
For applications with hundreds of entity types, **compiled models** (`dotnet ef dbcontext optimize`) cut EF startup/first-query time significantly by precompiling the model metadata. Combined with **`EnableRetryOnFailure`** for Azure SQL transient faults, these are the two config toggles most production EF apps want.

**Learn next:** only relevant at scale — measure startup first, apply if your model is large.

entries.title: Components that respond to their own size
entries.bodyMarkdown:
**Container queries** (`container-type: inline-size` + `@container`) solve what media queries can't: the same card component adapting to a wide main column vs a narrow sidebar — the viewport is identical, so media queries are blind. Now baseline in all modern browsers. Essential for a real component library.

**Learn next:** make one card component self-responsive with a container query instead of a media query.

entries.title: :has(), @layer, subgrid, clamp()
entries.bodyMarkdown:
- **`:has()`** — the 'parent selector' (style a card that contains an image)
- **`@layer`** — explicit cascade precedence, ends the specificity/`!important` arms race
- **Subgrid** — nested grids align to parent tracks (card internals lining up across cards)
- **`clamp()` / `min()` / `max()`** — fluid sizing that eliminates whole categories of breakpoints

**Learn next:** replace a fixed heading size with `clamp(1.5rem, 1rem + 2.5vw, 2.5rem)`.

entries.title: INP replaced FID as a Core Web Vital
entries.bodyMarkdown:
**Interaction to Next Paint (INP)** replaced First Input Delay in March 2024 — it measures worst-case responsiveness across the whole session, not just the first input. Target < 200ms. It's the event-loop lesson graded: long tasks and oversized change-detection cycles are the offenders.

**Learn next:** run Lighthouse (mobile, throttled), find your slowest interaction in the Performance panel, and chunk or defer the work behind it.

entries.title: Smooth animated transitions between states/pages
entries.bodyMarkdown:
The **View Transitions API** (`document.startViewTransition`) animates between DOM states — and now works for cross-document (multi-page) navigations too. Angular has router integration. Gives native-app-feeling transitions without heavy animation libraries, and it degrades gracefully.

**Learn next:** wrap a list→detail navigation in a view transition and watch it cross-fade.
